Microsoft has released updates to address critical vulnerabilities in Windows graphics rendering services.  Exploiting these vulnerabilities, a remote, unauthenticated attacker could execute arbitrary code or cause a denial of service on an affected system.

**Description**

The November 2005 Microsoft Security Bulletin addresses multiple buffer overflows in Windows image processing routines. Viewing a specially crafted image from an application utilizing a vulnerable routine could trigger these vulnerabilities. If the application can access images from remote sources, such as websites or email, remote exploitation becomes possible.

subject: Re: [Razor-users] Authentication Error
body:
I have some concerns about the Razor v2 release.  While the website states that the first beta is available, I believe the code is still very much pre-beta. 

The "Download" page instructs users to fetch the latest versions of razor-agents and razor-agents-sdk packages, but it's not clear these are development packages. The website's design is confusing and misleading.  The fact that the word "beta" appears in some introductory text on the front page isn't sufficient indication.  

It seems to me that Vipul and his team might be intentionally misleading users into downloading development code to gain more test sites.  Of course, this is just my opinion.

Regardless, the main point is that the current release doesn't appear to be production-ready.

subject: [DMDX] DMDX 3.1.0.0
body:
Today marks a significant milestone for DMDX with the introduction of 2D input support, hence the substantial version number update.

A new keyword, <2DInputDevice text1 text2,N1,N2,N3,N4 [...]>, allows you to designate a 2D input device, such as a touchscreen.  

* **text1:**  Must be the name of an existing, calibrated DirectInput device (unless it's "mouse", see below).  Once selected, this device cannot be deselected within the current item file without starting a new one using the chain keyword. You can, however, unmap all device buttons using the "." command. Names containing spaces require quotation marks.

* **text2:**  Provides an arbitrary name for a screen region that will trigger a response (with added "+" and "-" symbols for button presses and releases).

* **N1, N2, N3, N4:** Define the region's coordinates. N1 is the leftmost coordinate, N2 is the topmost, N3 is one more than the rightmost, and N4 is one more than the bottom (similar to a standard Windows RECT structure).  Repeat text2,N1,N2,

The White Drawing Room, initially intended as a temporary space, was erected without much consideration for aesthetics. It was originally planned for an Indian masquerade, a Lalla Rookh festival, in honor of Grand Duchess Charlotte, daughter of Frederick William III, and her husband, the future Tsar Nicholas I.

Another observation I made, besides the one-sidedness of education in the schools, was the prevailing tendency among young men planning their careers to aspire to government positions, always considering the legal profession, particularly that of a lawyer or judge, as the most honorable pursuit.

The only means of communication with the east were the two antiquated trestle bridges at Dirschau and Marienburg. An online "anarchist scavenger hunt" planned for next week's International Monetary Fund and World Bank annual meeting in Washington D.C. has sparked concern among police who worry about potential property damage and disruption. 

The scavenger hunt, proposed on the anarchist website www.infoshop.org, includes tasks like breaking a McDonald's window for 300 points, puncturing a police car tire for 75 points, and throwing a pie at a corporate executive or World Bank delegate for 400 points.

D.C. Assistant Police Chief Terrance Gainer revealed at a congressional hearing on Friday that law enforcement is discussing whether the planned protests, including the scavenger hunt, pose such a threat to security that proactive measures are necessary.

Thousands of demonstrators are anticipated to gather outside the IMF and World Bank headquarters next weekend. The Anti-Capitalist Convergence, a D.C.-based anarchist group, is also organizing a day-long traffic blockade, banner drops, and protests against major corporations in the downtown area.

Ron Chase

body:
Subject: Re: A few multiple dispatch questions

Hi,

I'm currently reviewing the multiple dispatch specification and have a few questions. I'll likely have more as I continue to process the information.

1) The spec states:

> A proto also adds an implicit multi to all routines of the same short name within its scope, unless they have an explicit modifier.

If you write:

> proto sub foo(:$thing) { ... }
> sub foo(Int $x) { ... }
> only sub foo() { ... }

Would this result in an error? This is because you've declared a method with 'only', but it can't be the only one due to the existing proto.

I believe this would be considered an error.

> multi sub foo(Int $blah) { ... } # 1
> proto sub foo(:$blah) is thingy { ... } # 2
> multi sub foo() { ... } # 3

subject: [ILUG] serial port transient failure
body:
I'm encountering a peculiar issue with my Evo N600c laptop's serial port while traveling.

While in the office, I successfully used the serial port to communicate with my mobile phone. However, upon arriving at my hotel, the port seemed to malfunction, behaving as if it were broken.  I initially suspected a problem with the serial drivers, so I rebooted into Windows and tested the port, which worked flawlessly. Returning to Linux, the issue persisted.

I explored various possibilities, including unstable power and electromagnetic interference, but nothing resolved the problem.  Even relocating the laptop to a different area within the hotel didn't help.  Frustrated, I resorted to using the IrDA port, which is typically unreliable.

Surprisingly, this morning back in the office, the serial port is functioning perfectly.

Has anyone encountered a similar issue with hotel environments affecting serial port functionality under Linux?  I'm curious to know if there's a specific technology at play that might be causing this strange behavior.


Cheers,
Waider.

subject: Radio 5CX News: Message of importance.
body:
This message is a collection of bizarre and seemingly unconnected statements.  

For example, we see a skyscraper giving a pink slip to a garbage can, a deficit inside a particle accelerator learning a lesson from cough syrup, and a dolphin hesitating about a wedge while a deficit inside a garbage can writes a love letter to a ball bearing sommelier.  

These sentences are juxtaposed with others that seem equally random, creating a surreal and nonsensical narrative. 

We also see recurring themes of objects interacting in unexpected ways, such as a vacuum cleaner dancing with a recliner, a power drill eating a buzzard, and a salad dressing finding lice on a sandwich. 

The overall effect is one of playful absurdity, challenging the reader to find meaning in the seemingly meaningless.

I downloaded a driver from the nVidia website and installed it using RPM.  I then ran Sax2, as suggested in some online postings, but my video card is still not listed. What should I do next?

To troubleshoot this, open a terminal and, as root, type `lsmod`. Look for a module called `NVdriver`. If it's not loaded, load it with the command `#insmod NVdriver.o`.  Remember to ensure this module is loaded on boot, otherwise you might encounter issues after rebooting.

Once the kernel module is loaded, open `/etc/X11/XF86Config` using `#vim`. In the "Driver" section, change "NeoMagic" to "nvidia".

subject: Re: sfio vs bio
body:
While sfio offers more comprehensive features, such as customizable error handlers for streams and integration with operating system functionalities like memory-mapped files, it's important to consider the scale of each library.  Sfio comprises approximately 7,000 lines of code (or 10,000 if you include stdio compatibility), significantly larger than bio's 700 lines.  

It's crucial to remember that size doesn't directly translate to performance or utility. Comparing sfio and bio is akin to comparing apples and kumquats – they serve different purposes and possess distinct strengths.

subject: Re: CD player UI for toddlers
body:
Subject: Re: CD player UI for toddlers

Mr. Fork writes:

Jim Whitehead suggests that for toddlers, pressing play should immediately start the music within half a second to establish a clear cause-and-effect relationship and prevent repeated button presses. Alternatively, a sound indicating imminent music playback could be helpful.

A tonal countdown might be a nice touch.

Jim also envisions the ideal toddler CD player as one that starts playing a CD instantly after insertion, similar to an MP3 player with solid-state storage.

I'm thinking, wouldn't it be great if all CD players had the ability to rip, encode, and cache the last few CDs inserted? This would eliminate seek delays during playback. After the initial ripping process, the laser pickup would only need to recognize the inserted CD, which might be faster than a traditional seek-and-start-at-first-track operation.

Furthermore, you could remove the CD while it's "playing."  

Perhaps if the CD is still in the cache, you wouldn't even need to insert it.  Imagine a step-through UI where you simply wave the CD at an electronic eye, and the player recognizes it based on the silkscreening. Kids would love that! They

I agree with Stephen's perspective. In my experience, the few times I've encountered difficulties installing current Linux distributions on Dell systems, it's been because Dell adopted new hardware, like Adaptec SCSI chipsets, before they were fully supported in the installation kernels. This is a risk you always take when purchasing hardware for Linux without first verifying chipset compatibility.  

Interestingly, Dell has generally caused fewer such issues compared to many other manufacturers over the years.

subject: [Spambayes] Deployment
body:
To move towards deployment, I propose creating a reusable tokenizer for email messages. Currently, the codebase lacks an easy-to-use and customizable tokenizer. The `tokenize()` function is straightforward: it takes a string and returns a stream of tokens, regardless of the output format (list, generator, tuple, etc.). All `tokenize()` functions in `GBayes.py` and `timtest.py` are interchangeable in this way.

However, we haven't explored whether a customizable tokenizer would be beneficial or how it could be helpful. This is a research question that requires further investigation.

The `timtest` module contains valuable practical knowledge about parsing email messages, but it wasn't designed for reusability. This is partly due to a lack of foresight. Extracting all tokenization knowledge would involve a significant amount of copy-pasting.  

Currently, changing from `timtoken import tokenize` to any other tokenizer is a simple process. Feel free to contribute an improved tokenizer if you wish.

I envision a module capable of tokenizing individual messages or collections of messages. The `Msg` and `MsgStream` classes in `timtest.py` provide a starting point, but their usefulness is limited by the diverse physical representations of

subject: Re: [Python-3000] Using *a for packing in lists and other  places
body:
I agree that "*yield" looks unappealing.  In a previous discussion, a suggestion was made to use "yield from x" to differentiate between iteratively yielding values from another iterator and yielding the values of an iterator as a tuple. While the parsing rules do distinguish these cases, I find the behavior not immediately obvious, and I anticipate the differences between `yield *x; yield *x(); yield *x,; yield (*x,); and yield *(x,)` becoming a source of confusion in Python in the future.

After following this thread, I believe this confusion is already a problem.  The potential for overlap with the other "*assign target" idea is too great.

While I'm fine with "item for *item,val in blah",  "*item for item in blah" is difficult to understand. Why not simply say "i2 for i1 in blah for i2 in i1"?  Yes, it's more verbose, but not frequently used enough to warrant the use of "*".  You can easily understand the logic by mentally translating it into nested for loops.  Plus, this approach works for any level of nesting. What happens when it's triple-nested?  Do we use "**"?  And

subject: Man dies as whale lands on boat
body:
A whale suddenly breached and crashed into the bow of a fishing boat off the coast of Port San Luis, California, tragically killing a restaurant owner named Jerry Tibbs. The 51-year-old from Bakersfield, California, was aboard his boat, The BBQ, when the impact threw him into the sea.  

Three other fishermen managed to remain on the damaged vessel, which was subsequently towed to shore by the US Coast Guard.  Authorities reported that Tibbs and his companions had been fishing for albacore when the accident occurred.  After an extensive 18-hour search, Tibbs' body was recovered. Coast Guard officials stated that this marked the first time they could recall an incident where a whale colliding with a boat resulted in a fatality.

subject: PAPER 8
body:
This email discusses three papers focusing on security vulnerabilities and defense mechanisms in peer-to-peer (P2P) networks.

The first paper, "The Sybil Attack" by John R Douceur, explores the inherent risk of redundancy in P2P systems. While redundancy can mitigate failures, malicious entities with sufficient resources can exploit it to gain significant control. The paper argues that centralized authorities can prevent Sybil attacks, but decentralized systems require unrealistic assumptions about attacker capabilities.  A key challenge is ensuring distinct identities correspond to distinct entities, as a single attacker controlling multiple identities can compromise data integrity and lead to leakage. The paper proposes a method for verifying entity separation through computationally intensive tasks, but acknowledges its limitations in preventing collusion and its reliance on a somewhat weak assumption about resource distribution.

The second paper, "Defending Against Eclipse Attacks on Overlay Networks" by Atul Singh, Miguel Castro, Peter Druschel, and Antony Rowstron, addresses the threat of Eclipse attacks, where attackers compromise a large portion of a node's neighbors, effectively isolating it. This attack can be launched through Sybil attacks or collusion and manipulates the overlay maintenance algorithm. The paper suggests bounding the indegree and outdegree of nodes to mitigate this attack.  A proposed defense mechanism

subject: Virgin's latest airliner.
body:
Virgin's latest airliner, the 130 million pound Airbus 340-600 named Claudia Nine after model Claudia Schiffer, has encountered an unexpected issue.  

The plane, which entered service just weeks ago, features a 5ft x 4ft mother and baby room equipped with a plastic changing table. However, this seemingly innocent amenity has become a target for passengers seeking to join the Mile High Club.  

Virgin has been forced to replace the table multiple times due to its misuse.  Initially, German engineers at Airbus were perplexed by the repeated damage. subject: [Spambayes] Deployment
body:
Guido,

I've reviewed your description of the program. It functions as both a POP client and server, allowing users to configure it with their actual POP servers. subject: Re: Config Register error
body:
I encountered the same issue you're facing. I couldn't get HBDL to successfully set the config register. To resolve it, I used DL to set it on one board and DLM on another, following the instructions in the HandyBoard technical reference manual: http://lcs.www.media.mit.edu/groups/el/Projects/handyboard/techdocs/hbmanual.pdf

After setting the config register using either method, I was able to utilize HBDL without problems.

If you're using DL or DLM on a Win95 machine, remember to run them in full-screen mode. Running them in a minimized window might appear to work initially, but you'll encounter communication errors when interacting with the board.

subject: rivulet: Dictionary.com Word of the Day
body:
Word of the Day for Tuesday, April 10, 2007

Today's word is **rivulet** (pronounced RIV-yuh-lut), a noun meaning a small stream or brook; a streamlet.

Here are some examples of how rivulet is used in literature:

* "But Stephen speaks of water in the desert, and triumphal swelling progress: raindrop, runnel, rivulet, river, sea." -- Blake Morrison, *As If*
* "There was a rivulet of scummy water heading for his highly polished black shoe." -- Joanne Harris, *Chocolat*
* "After two minutes in the steam chamber, sweat began to flow in rivulets from every pore in my body, dripping steadily from my fingertips." -- Fen Montaigne, *Reeling in Russia*

The word rivulet comes from Italian *rivoletto*, diminutive of *rivolo*, from Latin *rivulus*, diminutive of *rivus*, "a brook, a stream."

subject: Re[2]: Selling Wedded Bliss (was Re: Ouch...)
body:
Adam,

You mentioned that product bundling works, which is an interesting point.

You also said that this arrangement is similar to "normal" marriage, except the woman gets a man in a country with opportunities for herself and her children, and avoids certain negative choices.  

Let me be clear: we don't live in a fantasy world where women are solely defined by their domestic roles. Women are capable of being productive, independent individuals who are not limited to cooking, cleaning, or serving as sexual objects.  

Sometimes, and this might surprise you, men and women actually fall in love and choose to marry for companionship and mutual affection.  It's a concept that might seem idealistic, but it's a reality for many people.  

If a man approached my mother 25 years ago and tried to convince her to marry him based solely on her cleaning skills, we wouldn't be having this conversation.

These situations can work if both parties are aware that it's a transactional arrangement: he wants a maid, a companion, and a sexual partner without financial obligation, while she seeks a country with economic stability and a lower risk of AIDS.  It's a contract, not a marriage based on love.

subject: Re: [Python-Dev] pydoc works with eggs? (python-2.5.1)
body:
Neal Becker wrote:

> pydoc blew up when I tried to view the documentation for the pytools module, which is an egg:
>
> pydoc -p 8082
> pydoc server ready at http://localhost:8082/
> ----------------------------------------
>...
>
> I see that installing the egg unzipped fixes this. It looks to me that pydoc doesn't work with zipped eggs.

What's interesting is that pydoc *used* to work with zipped eggs. At least, help() did. The changes I made in 2.5 were primarily to ensure that help(package) would function with zipped eggs.

Based on the traceback, it appears that pydoc is attempting to parse comments from the source for an object without a docstring. I wasn't aware of this capability, so I never tested it. 



_______________________________________________
Python-Dev mailing list
zvllln-eum@python.org
http://mail.python.org/mailman/listinfo/python-dev
Unsubscribe: http://mail.python.org/mailman/options/python-dev/python-dev%40tangomu.

subject: Re: [SAtalk] How'd this one slip through?
body:
Rose,

Bobby wrote:

> Ouch ;-)  Although I'm not ready to give up razor just yet, I'd like to
> see a pyzor check in SA.  I don't think it even needs to be written in
> perl.  SA is actually calling razor-check and razor-report as well as
> dccproc for those checks so it should be nothing more than duplication
> of coding to add pyzor. 

While I understand the desire to add pyzor to SA, there's a potential issue with Python's startup time.  Historically, Python has had a longer startup time compared to Perl, which is one reason why it wasn't widely used for CGIs.  Although this might have changed recently, it's something to consider.

Matt.

subject: [opensuse] console keymap
body:
Hi,

I'm encountering a peculiar issue with keymap behavior in console programs on openSUSE 10.1. Specifically, keys like home, end, backspace, and delete aren't functioning as expected.

At the bash prompt, pressing home or end doesn't move the cursor to the start or end of the line. However, within vi, home and end work correctly.  

In vi's insert mode, backspace doesn't function at all. Delete works as long as there are characters on the line, but when the line is empty, it should delete the entire line and move to the next line, which isn't happening.

Interestingly, this problem doesn't occur in 10.3 when I'm directly connected. However, when I SSH from 10.1 to a 10.3 server, the same issue persists. I suspect a terminal-related problem, but I haven't been able to isolate the cause.


Best regards,
Nick Zeljkovic

subject: Re: It's a small world
body:
Subject: Re: It's a small world

Luis,

You mentioned Slummerville has actual broadband competition, including DSL.  I was curious if you actually tried to order it.  I've heard that a lot of advertised DSL service in Somerville can't actually be obtained. People I know who've tried have been told things like "no available copper" or "full DSLAM," and ended up with ATTBB, RCN, or even a T1 line.

I didn't bother trying DSL this time around. My past experiences with it haven't been great, so I went with AT&T digital cable and a cable modem instead.  Even the possibility of DSL here is better than what I had in the supposedly tech-friendly Triangle in NC.

Best,
Luis

subject: Re: can a sequence of events also be considered a hierarchy? - Music
body:
I'm reminded of music as a sequence of events with a hierarchical structure, encompassing bars, phrases, and melodies.  A recent article in Physica A offered a fascinating analysis of the temporal structure of folk songs. It makes me wonder if you're conflating the scientific *description* of music with music itself. Art, in its essence, transcends mere description of its components.

body:
The strategy for SoCal appears to be incrementalism, with both the Assembly (AB82xx) and Senate (SB 78xx) passing MOU bills, but in different versions that won't be reconciled until after the 30-day recess, if at all.  This delays disaster once again, as both bills will pass on a non-urgency basis, as predicted yesterday, meaning a 90-day delay for implementation.  This will likely result in another round of amendments, redrafts, and negotiations.

September 15th remains a critical deadline. Bank creditor forbearance is set to expire then, with only a small chance of extension. Additionally, FERC will likely have issued its refund ruling by this date, and it is unlikely to be favorable to California.

The worst-case scenario for SoCal is if Burton continues to push SB78xx after the recess.  For those interested in understanding why, please continue reading. Otherwise, stay tuned for more updates later today as the full Assembly and Senate meet to vote on the MOU bills and the budget.

Hi,

I'm running uw-imapd on my Debian Etch home server, serving mailboxes through squirrelmail using an SSL connection. Following squirrelmail's documentation, I installed imapproxy from the Debian repositories and configured it to use port 143 for connecting to the server and port 144 for squirrelmail connections. However, when I attempt to authenticate, I encounter an error. The log shows the following message:

IMAP_Line_Read(): Protocol error.  Line terminated by LF, not CRLF

I'm unsure if this is a bug or a configuration issue on my end.  My Google searches haven't yielded many results, with only a single unanswered post on this mailing list and a few scattered mentions. I'm at a loss as to where to begin debugging.

Could you offer any suggestions? It's possible that the issue lies with Debian's implementation or a specific tweak I made.  Perhaps the imapproxy community would have more insight into this problem.

I'm not a big fan of CS PhDs in general. They often seem to hinder practical coding skills, and the world doesn't need another treatise on theoretical computer science. Russell is one of the few exceptions I know, someone who can still write code after earning a PhD.

While I think AI programs at Stanford, MIT, or CMU are valuable exceptions, I'm not sure a traditional CS PhD is the best path.  GMU might be interesting, though. Perhaps a multi-agent/economics/AI program could be successful there, given the pool of advisors.

My hunch is that once the novelty of "multi-core" programming wears off, and people realize that Erlang or join- or pi-calculus are the future of concurrency, the intersection of game theory, economics, practical game playing, and heterogeneous multi-agent systems will become a hot topic. GMU could be as ripe for groundbreaking work in this area as any other institution outside of Hebrew University in Jerusalem.

Of course, this advice comes from someone who didn't even finish their undergraduate degree!

subject: panda ranks famous people used to spread computer viruses
body:
A recent coordinated attack using four types of malware, featuring an image of Michael Jackson, highlights the resurgence of using famous names to distribute malware online. This attack, detected by Panda Software as Downloader.dbr, involved mass-mailing messages worldwide, exploiting a fabricated story about Jackson's suicide attempt. This tactic exemplifies social engineering, leveraging celebrity popularity to spread malicious threats.

The malware's distribution strategy is intricate. The spammed message contains a link to a webpage. This page, identified by Panda Software as Phel.j, exploits a browser vulnerability to install Inor.ak, an HTML application, on the user's computer. This, in turn, grants access for Downloader.dbr to be installed. However, the malicious chain doesn't end there. Once installed, this Trojan downloads the Au variant of the Dedler worm, which carries out the actual malicious actions on the infected computer.

This isn't the first time Michael Jackson has been used as bait for malware distribution. Last November, a similar email circulated, supposedly offering a link to a video of Jackson with a child. This video was non-existent, but instead, a Trojan was downloaded onto the user's computer.

While complex, coordinated techniques like this are not common

You're asking about measuring battery capacity, which is how long your robot can run on its current charge.  I understand you're new to electronics, so let me try to explain.

subject: Re: Please help a newbie compile mplayer :-)
body:
Rob wrote that he downloaded gcc3 and libgcc3, but still encounters the same error message when attempting to rebuild or recompile using rpm. He mentioned doing this as root and downloading the files as root as well. He's seeking further assistance in understanding the issue.

I've never installed source rpms with apt, but if you're getting a "file not found" error, it might be because the source rpm was already installed. To check this, navigate to /usr/src/redhat/SPECS/. If you find a file named mplayer.spec, you can use the command "rpm -bb mplayer.spec" to create a binary build in /usr/src/redhat/RPMS/i386/.

subject: Re: [OT] Bogus MX opinions
body:
I don't employ a system like nolisting, as Richard described. My intention in posting was simply to raise the issue without sparking a heated discussion.  

I believe the MX retry strategy lacks clarity and consistency in its implementation, which inherently carries the risk of mail loss.  

Furthermore, a determined bot owner could easily circumvent any MX-related safeguards by utilizing multiple client connections. While I acknowledge that fake MX records can be effective, I question whether they necessitate constant monitoring to mitigate potential false positives.  

Statements like "I haven't encountered a single false positive" are unconvincing without concrete evidence and details about the methods used to verify this claim.

subject: Re: [opensuse] Reinstall KDE4.
body:
Subject: Re: [opensuse] Reinstall KDE4

Tom Cada wrote:

> I updated KDE4 to the latest version 4.01. Now, when I start it, none of the widgets (icons?) are displayed. There are just items showing a message that the object cannot be displayed. Additionally, there's no wallpaper or taskbar.

> One thought I have is that I never removed previous versions before installing the new one.

The switch from the KDE:KDE4 repository to the KDE:KDE4:STABLE:Desktop repository has initiated a new sequence of package release numbers. This means you need to update all packages from the KDE:KDE4:STABLE:Desktop repository, even if the already installed package appears newer.

> What's the best way to remove the existing installation so that a fresh install can be performed?

In Yast, you can set the filter to the KDE:KDE4:STABLE:Desktop repository. Then, you can choose to update all displayed packages, disregarding their package version and release.

Cheers,
Herbert

We're excited to announce the release of 'mixed', a new module designed to handle mixed numbers and fractions with ease.  

This module provides a dedicated class for parsing both string and float inputs, ensuring accurate arithmetic operations.

**Installation:**

* `easy_install mixed`

**Development Version:**

* `hg clone http://hg.assembla.com/mixed_pythonmixed_python`

**Example Usage:**

```python
>>> from mixed import Mixed
>>> m1 = Mixed('4 2/3')
>>> m2 = Mixed('4/10')
>>> m2.reduced()
Mixed('2/5')
>>> m1 + m2
Mixed('5 1/15')
>>> print m1 / m2
11 2/3
>>> Mixed(-1.5)
Mixed('-1 1/2')
>>> Mixed(2./3.)
Mixed('2/3')
```

subject: Re: [Python-Dev] [Python-3000] Code freeze?
body:
Christian Heimes inquired about documentation regarding attribute access restrictions.  

I confirmed that the restriction is documented in the development documentation, specifically in the section on special methods [1]. This information was backported from the Python 3000 documentation and is not included in documentation for versions 2.5 and earlier.

Essentially, special methods must be defined directly on a new-style class for the interpreter to reliably access them. While defining them on a new-style instance might influence the interpreter's behavior in certain situations, it's not guaranteed.

During my response preparation, I discovered why the new tempfile tests functioned correctly before I integrated them. I was testing on the trunk, where _TemporaryFileWrapper is a classic class. Classic class special method lookup doesn't utilize the 'direct-to-type' optimization, enabling those tests to work with the tempfile module as written for 2.x.

However, proxies like _TemporaryFileWrapper, which rely on __getattr__ to provide special methods, will not function properly when implemented as new-style classes.  They require explicitly defining the overridden special methods, similar to how SpooledTemporaryFile does.

This situation suggests a potential need for a -3 warning that identifies instances where "__*

subject: Re: Backtracking in Icon
body:
While backtracking might not always be the most efficient approach compared to optimized algorithms, it proves incredibly useful when a readily available non-backtracking solution isn't feasible within a reasonable timeframe.  

This is especially true in scenarios where execution speed isn't paramount, such as interactive programs where user interaction dominates the program flow.  

Ehud previously shared a code snippet demonstrating backtracking, originally authored by Dan Higdon back in 1988. This code, which I enhanced for a university course scheduling application, showcases the power of backtracking in a practical setting.

The application aimed to help students create personalized schedules in an environment with numerous course sections. The algorithm, while potentially daunting for those unfamiliar with Icon, backtracking, and recursive backtracking, becomes more understandable within those contexts.

It works by iteratively removing a class, scheduling the remaining classes recursively, and then attempting to find a suitable section for the removed class that adheres to various constraints. These constraints include factors like campus distance, class availability, and time conflicts.

If a suitable section is not found or the generated schedule is rejected by the student, the algorithm backtracks to explore alternative sections of the previously selected class.  

For classes with mandatory labs, the algorithm automatically incorporates them into

subject: [perl #46543] Strange behavior of map inside @{}
body:
This email reports a potential bug in perl, submitted by qxqoyvf-d7e@egg.flyinganvil.org using perlbug 1.35 running under perl v5.8.8.

The issue involves the behavior of the `map` function when used inside a hash reference. The reporter provides a minimal script demonstrating the unexpected results:

```perl
sub r { map [$_], 4; }
sub p { @{ shift; } }
print   'r = ', r(),
       ', @r = ', @{ r(); },
      ', p() = ', p( r() ),
  $/;


  print  "@{  +map [1, 2, 3, $_], (4); }" # prints nothing
  print "@${ \\+map [1, 2, 3, $_], (4); }" # prints '1 2 3 4'
```

The reporter notes that the behavior doesn't seem to change across different perl versions, with bleed@31847 being the latest tested.

The email also includes detailed configuration information for the reporter's perl v5.8.8 installation, including platform details

subject: Re: [Python-3000] Python source code on Bazaar vcs
body:
Subject: Re: [Python-3000] Python source code on Bazaar vcs

Barry Warsaw announced that the Python source code for versions 2.5, 2.6, and 3.0 is now publicly available through the Bazaar distributed version control system.

Thank you to Barry and the entire team for this great work!

Ubuntu users will need to install a newer version of bzr before they can check out the sources.  Here's how:

1. Open the `/etc/apt/sources.list.d/bzr.list` file with `sudo vi`.
2. Add the following lines:
   ```
   deb http://ppa.launchpad.net/bzr/ubuntu gutsy main
   deb-src http://ppa.launchpad.net/bzr/ubuntu gutsy main
   ```
3. Update your package lists with `sudo apt-get update`.
4. Install bzr, bzr-gtk, and bzrtools using `sudo apt-get --force-yes -y install bzr bzr-gtk bzrtools` (note the `--force-yes` flag as the packages are not yet signed).

For more information,

body:
I'm going to experiment with a different window manager to see if that resolves the issue of my exmh-unseen window going outside its designated area.  Interestingly, I don't encounter this problem with any other windows, only the exmh-unseen window.

Have you considered enabling the "Show unseen message count in folder cache" option? This feature displays the number of unseen messages next to each folder name (if there are any), eliminating the need for the unseen window unless you're utilizing multiple sequences beyond "unseen".

subject: Re: Python 2.2 site libs?
body:
Mark,

It's frustrating that Red Hat ships both Python 1.5.2 and 2.2 (as python2) without readily available RPMs for many of the libraries that work with 2.2.  

Specifically, I'm having trouble finding RPMs for modules like pgdb and xml.

Regards,
Panu

subject: Internet ^ Apothe
body:
Before a large gathering at the opening of the Elbe-Trave Canal, he famously declared, "The Central Canal must and will be built."  Our relationship remained harmonious until his retirement. Despite railway construction in western Germany, there were still significant gaps in the railway network, posing serious challenges for troop mobilization and deployment. These issues had long needed addressing.

After hours of work, the note was finally completed. It was meticulously reviewed, passed between hands several times, and read aloud by me half a dozen times before being signed.  England increasingly distanced itself from its promises, and no draft agreement was ever received.

It was His Excellency Miquel who encouraged me to support the ambitious canal projects and stood by me when Prussian Conservatives opposed the Central [Rhine-Weser-Elbe] Canal, ultimately leading to its failure. He strengthened the King's resolve, urging him to persevere until victory was achieved. Admiral Werner's "Book of the German Fleet" was one of the few works that could ignite the spirit of the German Empire. The Skagerrak (Jutland) battle demonstrated the fleet's true value and Tirpitz's brilliance.

The visit concluded successfully, with Londoners from all walks of life expressing their goodwill towards the King

subject: [DMDX] retrace rate determination
body:
This morning started off frustrating. It seems the issue with my home machines is the new NVIDIA drivers, specifically Detonator 3 version 618. TimeDX is struggling with them; they're performing actions that skew the benchmarks.

My other home machine, equipped with an 85Hz display, was accurately locking onto the retrace with the older drivers, reporting refresh intervals of 11.75ms.  After installing the new 618 drivers, things went haywire. Instead of consistently reporting longer retrace intervals like my primary machine (which prefers 12.00 unless timed for excessively long intervals, then dropping to around 11.8), the other machine now reports shorter intervals, around 11.6ms.

Even more concerning, the anti-cheating code I recently implemented is ineffective on that machine. At least on this machine, it prevents the cheating detection code from recognizing the issue (though it doesn't change anything else). It seems I'll need to implement another layer of anti-cheating, essentially a battle between my software and theirs. This is incredibly frustrating.

-jonathan (j.c.f.)

subject: Re: [Python-3000] The release process
body:
Christian,

Thanks for the clarification on the NEWS file process.  I appreciate you taking the time to explain it.

It's good to know that you, Guido, and others are actively monitoring the CVS lists and ensuring that bug fixes and new features are properly documented in Misc/NEWS.  Knowing this makes me feel much more comfortable as the Release Manager.

I understand that Misc/NEWS isn't a major concern, but Docs/whatsnew/ is.  It seems that only a few people, primarily Georg and Andrew, are responsible for updating these files.

While it's okay if these files lag behind during the alpha stages, it would be beneficial to start focusing on getting them in shape as we approach beta releases.

Thanks again for your insights!

Best regards,
-Barry

[Python-Dev] Introducing the ``make check`` command
body:
Subject: [Python-Dev] Introducing the ``make check`` command

I recently wrote a script to run `reindent.py` over all .py files before they are checked in, as I've had too many commits rejected due to trailing whitespace.  Since there are other considerations when creating a patch, the script also notifies users if they've edited files in the Doc directory, Misc/ACKS, or Misc/NEWS.

I'd like to enhance this command with a few features.  

First, I'd like it to detect if any files (excluding those in the Doc directory) have changed since the last `regrtest` run. This might require writing out a file to track modification times, but at least we could use `stat` to get the modification time.  Do you have any concerns about this approach?

Second, I want to establish a clear rule about whether a patch requires adding someone to Misc/ACKS. I could use `svn diff` to analyze the changes and determine if they warrant an update to the file.  The output could then be written to a common file, eliminating the need to repeatedly run the command for the same patch.

Finally, I'd like to add trailing whitespace stripping

subject: Re: [Python-3000] range() issues
body:
Mark,

I understand your point about the potential performance issues with `range()` for very large numbers.  You're right, if `n` is in the hundreds of millions, it might be worth considering alternatives like C. However, for many situations, the convenience of Python outweighs the minor performance hit, especially if it means I can focus on other tasks while the loop runs.

To illustrate, I tested `for i in range(2**31): pass` on my 32-bit laptop and it took about 12 minutes. Interestingly, `for i in range(2**31-1): pass` was significantly faster at 131 seconds, suggesting there might be room for optimization.

It's also worth noting that `range(n)` currently works in Py3k even for `n` exceeding `sys.maxsize`.  What's the reasoning behind potentially breaking this functionality?

While I agree that a 64-bit OS combined with an extension might be beneficial for handling truly massive datasets, my typical use cases involve long-running processes rather than datasets with significant space and time requirements.

subject: DeMorgan's Law doesn't work
body:
I'm trying to find the first filename matching the pattern "w[0-9][0-9]" that's unused in two directories. 

My current code works:

```
nn := (0 to 9) || (0 to 9),
not exists( downloadDir || "w" || nn ),
not exists( targetDir   || "w" || nn )
)|stop( "No names of the form w[0-9][0-9] were unused." )
```

However, the separation of the two "not exists" calls feels clunky, reminiscent of Algol-family languages' need for explicit disjunctions like `if importantNumber = 1 | importantNumber = 2`.  Snobol-family languages handle this more elegantly with `importantNumber = 1 | 2`.  

Ideally, Icon should allow factoring out the two "not exists" calls.  

I tried this:

```
nn := (0 to 9) || (0 to 9),
not exists( (downloadDir|targetDir) || "w" || nn )
)|stop( "No names of the form w[0-9][0-9] were unused

subject: [Bug 5673] [review] "ALL" header includes extra spaces between header names and values
body:
Subject: [Bug 5673] [review] "ALL" header includes extra spaces between header names and values

http://issues.apache.org/SpamAssassin/show_bug.cgi?id=5673

------- Additional Comments From user7@gvc.ceas-challenge.cc  2007-10-10 02:59 -------
(In reply to comment #6)
> What's the problem here?  The amount of whitespace is irrelevant.

I disagree with this assessment. If the original headers appear as:

  Header1: value1
  Header2: value2

then `get("ALL")` should not return:

  Header1:  value1
  Header2:  value2

with extra spaces added between the header names and their values. While I understand the potential benefit of simplifying excessive whitespace to a single space, adding spaces where they don't exist in the original string is, in my opinion, a bug.

subject: A Non-Integer Power Function on the Pixel Shader
body:
From Wolfgang Engel's "ShaderX" book published by Wordware, this feature showcases a clever shader trick. It provides a per-pixel approximation of a non-integer power function, achieving good results for input values ranging from 0 to 1.  

The technique is notable for its ability to handle large exponents and its lack of reliance on texture look-ups.  Furthermore, its scalability allows for optimization: more instructions can be dedicated to reducing the error or achieving higher exponents.

subject: Re: Truth-Table generator
body:
You mentioned wanting a more general truth table generator that could handle more than two variables (t1 and t2).  

While extending the code for a fixed number of variables is possible, it becomes increasingly complex. Here's an example:

```
procedure main()

   every (t1 := (0|1)) & (t2 := (0|1)) & (t3 := (0|1)) do {
      every writes(format( t1 | t2 | t3			# variables
                           "|" |			# separator
                           iand(t1,t2) | ior(t1,t2) |	# functions
			   ixor(t1,t2) | iand(t1, ior(t2,t3)) |
                           ior(iand(t1,t2),iand(t1,t3))
                  ))
      write()
      }

end
```

There are techniques to generalize this approach for 'n' variables using generators, but I've forgotten the specifics. I was hoping someone would take the existing solution and expand it.  

If I recall correctly, those techniques were quite intricate.

subject: opd mods
body:
Subject: opd mods

Hi Bill,

I've made some modifications to the focus command in the optical pointing daemon and wanted to share the details.  I've added two new parameters to the /etc/opdrc file:

* **BACKLASH0:** This parameter, previously known as the term used to date, has had its sign changed from -2000 to 2000 to evaluate its impact.
* **BACKLASH1:** This is the relaxation term you requested, and it should have the same sign as BACKLASH0.

You can edit the opdrc file directly on kueppers as smtobs, and the opd will automatically reread the changes without requiring a restart.

The focus command now has only two options, as shown in the following help response:

```
help focus
  focus [-a] [-m steps]
    opt a       - autofocus mode
    opt m       - manual focus mode
      steps     - number of steps to move
```

Currently, the manual focus mode utilizes the new backlash terms, while the autofocus mode does not. I'm still working on refining the autofocus mode modifications.

subject: A Living Memorial
body:
The Wall Street Journal's recent article, "A Living Memorial," raises a crucial point: the best tribute to those lost in the World Trade Center tragedy is not a static monument, but a vibrant, thriving city.  

The article rightly laments the current focus on the size and scope of the memorial, arguing that it overshadows the urgent need to rebuild downtown Manhattan and restore its economic vitality. The loss of 100,000 jobs following the attacks cannot be ignored, and the city's political and economic leaders should prioritize the return of commerce to the area.

The article criticizes the commission tasked with overseeing the site's redevelopment for prioritizing the memorial over rebuilding. It suggests a more pragmatic approach, similar to the Pentagon's strategy: rebuild first and then dedicate a smaller space for a memorial.

Furthermore, the article highlights the influence of anti-development activists who seek to transform the World Trade Center site into a vast park. This, it argues, would be a disservice to the memory of those who perished, as it would perpetuate the image of the site as a cemetery rather than a symbol of resilience and renewal.

subject: Re: fatal: bad string length 0 < 1: virtual_mailbox_base
body:
Wietse Venema suggested checking the output of `postmap -s hash:/etc/postfix/transport` to see if there were any issues with the /etc/postfix/transport file.  

The output of that command showed the following:

```
csadm.just.gov.ab.ca            smtp:[192.168.133.177]
ntsjam.just.gov.ab.ca           smtp:[192.168.133.177]
```

Despite this, mail was still being sent to the virtual delivery agent.  Wietse suggested using the following command to see if any other files might be causing the issue:

```
find /etc/postfix -type f -print | grep -v virtual | xargs strings -f | grep virtual
```

He also suggested finding the queue file and using `postsuper -r queueid` to investigate further.

This email is unrelated to the previous discussion about the `fatal: bad string length 0 < 1: virtual_mailbox_base` error.

subject: Walking Robot Question
body:
I'm curious about how servos are controlled in a walking robot.  

It seems to me that multiple legs would need to move simultaneously and in perfect coordination.  If servos were adjusted individually, one leg would likely drag the others, making walking impossible.  A control system that can manage multiple servos but only changes them one at a time doesn't appear suitable for this task.

What type of controller would be used for this kind of intricate movement?  Have I missed any crucial aspects of this process?

body:

When I attempt to uninstall a package using `rpm -e [rpm package]`, I receive an error stating that the package is not installed.  However, when I try to install it with `rpm -i [rpm package]`, I get an error indicating that the package is already installed.

To overcome this, I force the installation using `rpm -i --force [rpm package]`. This successfully installs the package, but when I try to uninstall it again, I still get the "package not installed" error.

How can I resolve this? I need to get RPM to recognize that the package is indeed installed and allow me to uninstall it properly. 


Thanks in advance,

Brian French -French

subject: Re: [Python-Dev] [Python-3000] The release process
body:
Christian Heimes asks why we need a release tool at all, pointing out that the 3.0a2 release process involved a lot of manual intervention. While he acknowledges the effort put into welease, he wonders if the time spent on it could have been better used fixing bugs.

He asks which Release Manager (RM) Christian is referring to, as Barry was hoping for cost-effective process support, which didn't materialize. However, welease is Anthony's RM process, and he likely saw it as an investment in quality and efficiency.

The fact that Barry found Anthony's process unusable doesn't necessarily reflect poorly on either Barry or Anthony's code. Release processes tend to be very personal, even within the same project.  The author's experience with XEmacs, which has three concurrent release processes, illustrates this point.  Each process has had different RMs who have all used their own tools, with no adoption of previous tools.

The author concludes by sharing their experience with release processes, emphasizing their personal nature.

subject: [DMDX] Re: RE: Registry Key Error
body:
Hi Pamela et al.,

I'm happy to report that I've resolved the registry key error.  

The issue stemmed from an incorrect video mode setting. I mistakenly assumed the video mode should match my computer's display settings (800x600, 16-bit color). However, my script was expecting 640x480, 8-bit.  

After selecting this mode in "Basic Tests>Set Video Mode" and clicking the "Just Select It" button, the script worked as expected. It's important to note that simply "testing" the mode wasn't sufficient; the "Just Select It" step was crucial for me.

Mike Dobbs
Senior Project Coordinator
Department of Psychology
Claremont Graduate University

subject: Re: B&W G3 instability
body:
Thanks for the detailed information about compatible DIMMs for the B&W G3. It's very comprehensive!

Unfortunately, I'm working with a collection of old DIMMs from antistatic bags, so I'm not familiar with their specific sizes and capabilities. I removed my three 256MB DIMMs and tried a PC100 256MB and a couple of 128MB DIMMs I had on hand. The 128MB DIMMs didn't work at all – the Mac wouldn't even power on with them installed. However, the 256MB DIMM worked fine, and the machine was stable with it.

I then took two 128MB DIMMs from another stable machine, and now I have a stable Mac with 512MB and two ATI cards. That's a good start!  

Now I'll need to start sourcing more DIMMs from old PCs to reach my goal of 640MB. I'll also try to get the other Mac up to at least 512MB if possible. 


Thanks again for your guidance!

subject: [lwv-healthcare] Millions with Chronic illness go untreated - NY Times
body:
Millions of Americans with chronic illnesses, such as diabetes and high blood pressure, are not receiving adequate treatment due to a lack of health insurance. This is the key finding of a new study published in the medical journal Annals of Internal Medicine, which provides the first comprehensive look at the health of the uninsured population.

The study, based on government health surveys of adults aged 18 to 64, estimates that approximately one in three working-age adults without insurance in the United States has been diagnosed with a chronic illness. Many of these individuals are forgoing doctor's visits or relying on emergency rooms for their medical care.

According to the report, about 11 million out of the 36 million uninsured Americans in 2004, the latest year of the study, had received a diagnosis for a chronic condition. Dr. Andrew P. Wilper, the study's lead author, emphasizes that with modern therapies, these conditions can be effectively managed. He points out that treatments for diabetes and hypertension are readily available and routine, provided insurance coverage is in place.

subject: Re: porting unix progs to plan9
body:
I'm concerned that simply porting *nix programs to Plan 9, just for the sake of familiarity, might detract from the unique strengths of Plan 9's toolset. 

In areas where Plan 9 lacks specific functionality, like a news reader, I believe it's more beneficial to develop new applications that leverage Plan 9's superior programming environment. For instance, I've started outlining a calendar management file system, and dhog is working on a news reading file system that will seamlessly integrate with other Plan 9 tools, particularly help(1).

P.S.  help(1) is fantastic. Spend an hour with it, and you'll never want to go back.  There are even rumors of an even more powerful successor called acme.

subject: chevron and texaco to merge
body:
Chevron Corp. and Texaco Inc. have announced plans to merge, forming ChevronTexaco Corp.  

Under the terms of the $100 billion merger, Texaco shareholders will receive 0.77 shares of Chevron common stock for each share they own. Existing Chevron shareholders will retain their current shares. This exchange ratio equates to approximately $64.87 per Texaco share, based on Chevron's closing stock price of $84.25 on October 13th. This represents an 18% premium based on Texaco's closing share price on the same date and a 25% premium based on the average relative share prices of the two companies over the 30-day period leading up to October 13th.

Following the merger, Chevron shareholders will own approximately 61% of the combined company's equity, while Texaco shareholders will hold about 39%. The newly formed ChevronTexaco will boast an enterprise value exceeding $100 billion.

The combined entity will possess substantial resources, including 11.2 billion barrels of oil equivalent (boe) in reserves, daily production of 2.7 million boe, and assets valued at $77 billion. Operations

subject: re : implementing term - structure of correlations for power
body:
Tanya,

While there are seasonal correlations in power, particularly for NP-15 and SP-15 (within the same region), the term structure of correlations can be input. However, the same correlation structure with similar periodicity might not apply between NP-15 and, for example, RLB (Neepool), although we would expect a seasonal relationship (summer/winter) with increased noise.

Even if we implement a correlational term structure for power, different rules would need to be inputted for different regions.

Naveen

---

Vladimir,

Regarding the implementation of the term structure of correlations for power:

1. Correlations are periodic with a period of one year. This means we can utilize 12 correlation matrices calculated from the first 12 forward contracts and apply these matrices to other forward months.

2. Using a decay factor can make the curves smoother.

3. Implementing multiple correlation matrices will not significantly impact the speed of calculations in the VAR model.

Please provide your response.

Thanks,

Tanya

subject: Re: [SAtalk] O.T. Habeus -- Why?
body:
On August 28, 2002, Daniel Quinlan wrote in response to Dan Kohn's suggestion that he could easily adjust Habeas scores locally:

> I think I've gained enough experience with SA to understand how to localize a score. However, I'm uncomfortable using SpamAssassin as a platform to promote your business at the expense of our user base.

Daniel agrees with this sentiment. He believes that if Habeas fails solely because of SA's lack of support, it reflects a serious flaw in Habeas's business model.  He suggests that Habeas should incentivize SA users to give it a chance.

On August 29, 2002, Justin Mason responded, stating that he doesn't see a problem with supporting Habeas in SpamAssassin but acknowledges Dan's concerns. Justin points out that high scores from SpamAssassin might be due to its current tendency to flag legitimate newsletters, requiring Habeas to lower the score. He emphasizes the need to address high scores in general, as spam should never exceed a score of 5 in the SpamAssassin system. Justin also mentions that other MTA plugins, like amavisd and MIMEDefang, already reject messages exceeding certain thresholds, highlighting the potential

subject: Re: [opensuse] Cooling Problems
body:
Subject: Re: [opensuse] Cooling Problems

Basil Chupin wrote:

> Philippe Landau wrote:
>> Basil Chupin wrote:
>>  
>>> Per Jessen wrote:
>>>    
>>>> Also, I would have thought the heatsink supplied by AMD for the Phenom
>>>> would be sufficient for 4 cores - but maybe not.         
>>> Nope. They provide the minimum required, but it's ultimately your responsibility to ensure the correct heatsink is installed for your specific environment.
>>>
>>> Cooling, heatsinks, and thermal pastes are a specialized field. Check out websites like Overclockers.com for in-depth information on heatsinks, temperatures, lapping heatsinks, and the right compounds to use. It's a fascinating world! :-) .
>>>     
>> Are you saying standard thermal paste can cause problems?
>>   
>
> It depends on what you consider "standard paste." There was once a common white paste used when CPUs generated less heat, but now there are advanced options containing silver particles (like Arctic brand, which I recently used) for better heat transfer from the CPU to the heatsink.

However, it's more than just the paste.

subject: Wall Following
body:
Hi All,

For the past three months, I've been grappling with a major challenge for my dissertation project. As a third-year undergraduate at the University of Sheffield, England, I'm working on "Autonomous Mobile Robotics." My goal is to construct a robot using a Fischertechnik kit and control it with a 6.270 board. The project has two main objectives: first, to enable the robot to follow walls, and second, to develop a Self-Organising Neural Network representation of its environment.

The problem lies with the infra-red sensors. I've dedicated numerous weeks to getting the robot to follow walls using infra-red, along with various other methods, but without success. The infra-red sensors seem either too temperamental or overly sensitive to be reliable. Has anyone else encountered similar difficulties? Any insights or experiences you could share would be greatly appreciated.

Regards,

Chris

body:
## Western Issues Update 9 - 04 - 2001

**For questions or comments on this report, please contact G. Britt Whitman at ext. 54014 or Kristin Walsh at ext. 39510.**

**Executive Summary**

* Northwest smelters are vying for favorable electricity rates.
* BC Hydro's rate freeze remains in effect.
* BPA rates are expected to rise, with California refunds doubtful.

**Aluminum Smelters (DSI's)**

On October 1, 2001, the long-term agreement between DSI's and BPA for preferred rates will end as the Bonneville Power Administration's new rate case takes effect. After 2001, DSI's will become discretionary customers of BPA.  

DSI's have been strategically "migrating" their load to local utilities in 9.9 MW increments to avoid BPA's new Large Single Load (NSL) designation and losing their preferred rate status. However, they are now quietly returning to BPA's federal system, and BPA officials have been willing partners in circumventing their own NSL regulations, established in 1980.

subject: ping... ping... ping...
body:
Subject: ping... ping... ping...

I'm reaching out to gauge the current state of Plan 9 deployments outside of Bell Labs. 

Specifically, I'm interested in understanding:

* How many sites have successfully installed and are running a usable Plan 9 system?
* Of those, how many still maintain a dedicated Plan 9 partition or machine?
* How many users at those sites regularly log into or use Plan 9 as their primary system?

I'm also curious about the configurations being used:

* What hardware platforms are people running Plan 9 on (e.g., SPARCS, x86)?
* Are dedicated file servers or WORM drives being employed?

Looking ahead, I'd like to explore the potential for a Plan 9 archive site.

* Would you prefer to access such a site using 9P or FTP?
* What types of files would you want to upload and download?

Finally, I'd appreciate insights into Plan 9 usage at your site:

* How many people use Plan 9?
* How many are involved in development or research?
* What specific projects are they working on?

subject: Re: [Python-Dev] trunk buildbot status
body:
To help troubleshoot the buildbot issue, I suggest navigating to your trunk root directory and running Tool\buildbot\build.bat. This script will automatically download all necessary dependencies and initiate the build process using vcbuild.  Building through the command line sometimes requires more attention than using Visual Studio, which usually handles things correctly.

I recently built the trunk on a Windows XP x86 machine with only Visual C++ Express 2008 installed. While I encountered errors with sqlite, tcl, db-4.4.20, and ssl, the interpreter successfully built and appears to run without issues. However, since I don't have bsddb installed, I wasn't able to execute the specific build step that's causing problems.

I can install bsddb within the next 24 hours if needed.

subject: Re: One scam to rule them all..
body:
You know, I was reading about a scam that originated in Nigeria, but instead of targeting bereaved widows, it focused on the supposed heirs of Sir Francis Drake's estate.  The con involved convincing people they were chosen as heirs and needed to contribute money to a fellow "heir" (who was actually the scammer) to reclaim the fortune from the Court of England.  Apparently, this scam, started by Oscar Hartzell back in the early 1800s, was quite successful.  Sound familiar?

Interestingly enough, I'm currently reading a book called "Drake's Fortune" by Richard Raynor about this very scam.  

Even though I'm pretty familiar with the 419 scam (I even wrote about it in a FoRK post a few months back: http://www.xent.com/pipermail/fork/2002-May/011928.html), I never realized it was connected to this older scam.  

I guess I'm not very good at recognizing patterns.

-Mike

http://xent.com/mailman/listinfo/fork

subject: Re: Broken OO-parent-method call & can()
body:
Linda,

You mentioned that you don't agree with the use of dispatch tables, but you do believe that a reference to an undefined subroutine should override a proper OO method call.

You explained that the original jump table example referred to all package local calls, making all entries valid. However, when the handling routines were split into separate packages or classes, the jump table stopped functioning. You acknowledge that it makes sense for the jump table not to work across packages or classes.

You also stated that the issue isn't related to packages or classes, but rather to the execution of \\& before the subroutine definitions are compiled. You seem to be struggling to understand my point; are you agreeing that a dispatch table should work in this scenario?

You mentioned that forward declarations must affect method resolution, citing an example from http://perlmonks.org/?node_id=647781. You believe that a defined forward reference should affect method resolution, and that in OO implementations, before returning a "method is undefined" error, it should look through the parent classes.

subject: [9fans] rfork(), getss() etc etc
body:
The fact that `clone()` doesn't split the stack in Linux is a feature, not a bug, and likely not worth dwelling on.  It's interesting, though, that FreeBSD's `rfork()` adopted `clone()`'s semantics, despite the somewhat misleading nature of the FreeBSD manual page. I recently heard that the manual page might be updated to clarify that `rfork()` is essentially `clone()` in name only. Apparently, FreeBSD's VM architecture prevents stack splitting, according to those "in the know."

So, what's the problem with this approach?  

Firstly, the only way to determine if you're the parent or child process after the split is to examine the system call's return value. This inevitably leads to the need for assembly code to establish a new stack, a step backward in my opinion. It's concerning to see open-source systems promoting assembly code usage through system call variants that are practically unusable without it.

Secondly, the newly created stack isn't managed or protected by the kernel. This is reminiscent of the criticisms leveled at Windows 9x and MacOS.

Thirdly, process identification relies solely on calling `getpid()`, rather than referencing a data structure

subject: [UAI] Final CFP: ML for Surveillance & Event Detection @ ICML-2006
body:
**Workshop Description**

The task of monitoring routinely collected data for anomalous events is common across various domains. This inspection, often referred to as event detection, serves purposes ranging from security surveillance, where a guard analyzes video footage for intrusions, to scientific data analysis, where scientists identify anomalies leading to new discoveries.  Event detection holds immense potential for a wide range of real-world applications, spanning security, finance, public health, medicine, biology, environmental science, manufacturing, astrophysics, business, and economics.

Traditionally, humans manually examined collected data for event detection. However, the increasing volume and complexity of data, driven by advancements in computing and global interconnectivity, necessitate more efficient solutions. While simple data types like univariate time series can be effectively monitored using established techniques like regression, Box-Jenkins models, and statistical quality control, more complex data, including multivariate records, images, video footage, audio recordings, spatial and spatio-temporal data, text documents, and relational data, pose new challenges.

Existing machine learning paradigms often struggle to address the unique characteristics of event detection applications. Standard supervised, unsupervised, semi-supervised, and active learning methods frequently fall short due to the specific nature of these problems. Consequently, the development of novel algorithms is crucial to fill this

subject: Cyberspace in the 21st Century: Part Seven, Security is Relative
body:
While security in cyberspace presents unique challenges compared to traditional computer systems, it also offers a different perspective.  Unlike systems crucial for commerce, where near-perfect security is paramount, cyberspace entertainment can tolerate a degree of "vandalism" and still remain enjoyable for the majority of users.  

This means our security goals are more flexible. We aim for a system that is roughly 90 percent clean, rather than striving for the unattainable 99.99 percent purity required in critical systems.  While we will continue to combat corruption, our target for system reliability is more achievable.

subject: EPA Stunned: Diesel Exhaust Can Cause Cancer
body:
The Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has concluded, after a decade of research, that inhaling diesel exhaust from large trucks and other sources can cause cancer in humans. This finding, released Tuesday, is expected to strengthen the government's efforts to reduce truck tailpipe emissions by mandating cleaner-burning engines and ultra-low sulfur diesel fuel.

While acknowledging uncertainties about the long-term health effects of diesel exhaust exposure, the EPA report, based on both animal studies and occupational exposure data, suggests a strong link between diesel exhaust and cancer risk in humans. The report emphasizes that this hazard likely extends to environmental exposure levels as well.

This conclusion aligns with previous findings from various world health agencies and studies conducted in California. Its significance lies in the EPA's role as the federal agency responsible for regulating diesel emissions under the Clean Air Act.

The report's release comes amidst concerns that the Bush administration might weaken Clinton-era regulations aimed at stricter emissions standards for large trucks and the near-elimination of sulfur in diesel fuel. EPA Administrator Christie Whitman has repeatedly assured the public that these tougher truck and diesel rules will be implemented. Last month, with White House approval, the EPA rejected attempts by some diesel engine manufacturers to delay these requirements, imposing new penalties on those who

subject: US-CERT Cyber Security Alert SA06-075A -- Adobe Macromedia Flash Products Contain Vulnerabilities
body:
**US-CERT Cyber Security Alert SA06-075A -- Adobe Macromedia Flash Products Contain Vulnerabilities**

Critical vulnerabilities have been discovered in Adobe Macromedia Flash Player and related software. These vulnerabilities could allow an attacker to gain control of your computer.

Affected systems include Microsoft Windows, Mac OS X, and other operating systems that utilize the following products:

* Adobe Macromedia Flash Player
* Adobe Macromedia Flash
* Adobe Macromedia Flex
* Adobe Macromedia Breeze
* Adobe Macromedia Shockwave Player

Exploiting these vulnerabilities could enable an attacker to take control of your computer or cause it to crash if you open a malicious Flash file, potentially hosted on a website.

Adobe has released updates to address these vulnerabilities. US-CERT strongly recommends updating to the latest version of your Macromedia product. For more technical details, please refer to US-CERT Technical Cyber Security Alert TA06-075A.

For updates, visit the Macromedia Security Bulletin.

**References:**

* US-CERT Technical Cyber Security Alert TA06-075A.html
* US-CERT Vulnerability Note VU#945060
* Macromedia

subject: Re: [python-win32] Idle Event
body:
Hi,

I was wondering how to run a function when the event loop is idle. This is for animation and some layout calculation purposes. Currently, I'm using threads, but it's impacting the responsiveness of my application.

To answer my own question: use `win32gui.PumpWaitingMessages`, which utilizes `peekmessage`.

You can then replace `PumpMessages()` with:

```python
while PumpWaitingMessages() == 0:
   #some idle function or processing here
```

~gvz

subject: Re: sa-learn weirdness...
body:
Subject: Re: sa-learn weirdness...

I agree that sa-learn remembers previously learned messages.  Once all old messages have been processed, you should only feed it new arrivals since the last sa-learn run. There's no need to keep 180 days worth of ham and spam in the temp folder!

To manage this, I rotate learning mailboxes to historical names monthly.  Each user has SpamAssassin-SPAM and SpamAssassin-HAM folders that sa-learn processes nightly. On the first of each month, these folders are moved to names like SpamAssassin-x-YYYYMM.

Furthermore, the script that launches sa-learn only informs it about learning folders that have been modified in the last three days.

subject: Football players addicted to video football
body:
The article explores the surprising phenomenon of professional football players' addiction to video football games.  They see it as a form of wish-fulfillment, allowing them to "manage" their virtual teams and escape the authority of their real-life coaches and bosses. This fascination mirrors the unexpected success of games like The Sims, which on the surface seem mundane.  

The article suggests that The Sims' appeal lies in its ability to let players tweak their virtual lives, exploring alternative paths and "trying on" different realities.  The idea of football players playing themselves in licensed video games is described as "neat and recursive," drawing a parallel to a Simpsons episode where Mr. Burns encounters Krusty buying Krusty-O's, highlighting the amusing self-referential nature.

The article then delves into the personal experience of NFL player Carr, who was surprised to see himself in a video game for the first time while shopping at Best Buy.  It emphasizes that this isn't just a phenomenon confined to young gamers, as even professional athletes, including massive offensive linemen, engage in intense competition in games like Madden NFL 2003 and Halo: Combat Evolved.
